turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: We'll hear argument next in No. 90-622, Florida against Luz Piedad Jimeno. Mr. Neimand, you may proceed whenever you are ready.

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The respondents were lawfully stopped in Miami, Florida for a traffic violation, and during that stop they were advised that they were under suspicion for transporting illegal narcotics in their automobile. Thereafter they were asked for consent to search their automobile for that narcotics. Pursuant to that request the consent was granted and the officer went directly into the passenger compartment and went to the brown paper bag, which was a grocery bag, unrolled the paper bag and found the cocaine in question. The lower court found these facts. The lower court also found that it could have been assumed by the respondents that after they were advised that they were looking for narcotics, that the officer would have gone directly to the bag in question. The court found, though, that it was bound by the Florida Supreme Court's prior decision in State v. Wells that it did not allow — that consent did not allow the officer to open a container within the passenger compartment. This court traveled directly through the lower court, found again the same findings of facts, and also found the same conclusion of law. The Florida Supreme Court without argument affirmed on the basis of State v. Wells. This Court then accepted certiorari jurisdiction. It is the state's contention in this case that the scope of the — of a consent is based upon the request that is asked and the knowledge that the officer gets from that. Therefore, in a situation such as this where they are informed that they are looking for cocaine, or drugs, as the case may be, they are further informed that they do not have to consent if they do not wish. They were further informed that if they did consent to the search that during the course of the search that they could choose at any time to terminate that search. And in this case no actions were ever taken by the respondents to terminate that search.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Neimand, what if the automobile had contained a locked briefcase? How would you interpret the consent then?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: If that is a -- locked with a key, then the, it probably would not be reasonable to assume that the individual consented to have his property broken. Under the theory of this Court of consent, the consent is a voluntary nature to allow the citizenry to help police ferret out crime, and it would not be reasonable, the state submits, to assume —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: May I ask then, are you agreeing that the Wells case was correctly decided?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: Only because the individual Wells was not present at that time.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So you do agree Wells was correctly decided?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: Only on its facts.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But at least on its facts.

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: On its facts, yes. That's why we did not —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And that's the only authority that this — the Florida court relied on in this case?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: When you answered Justice O'Connor and you said that it's unreasonable to assume that there would be consent to break the lock, did you mean that in the technical sense of picking it or actually destroying it? Suppose the police officer was a good lock picker and he could just pick the lock?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: I think that almost goes to the same that if it was just clasp shut and you have a attache case where you could just —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No. My hypothetical is this is a good lock, but he's a good lock picker.

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: Well, if he doesn't destroy the property. See, that's the key, the destruction of the property. I don't think any reasonable person would consent to have his property destructed. I think he would be --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What about to simply have his lock picked?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: Well, he wouldn't consent to have the lock picked, but it would not violate the reasonableness because he had no property destruction.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I know, but it goes to what the scope of his consent was.

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: The scope would be determined by what he was asked to look for.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, where do you come out on the question? Does he consent or does — is the search lawful or is it not lawful if there is a strong lock but the office is able to pick it?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: In terms of a reasonableness approach, that would probably be unlawful as to the scope of the search because there was a protection interest involved and there would be somewhat of a destruction of the property.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How about opening a woman's handbag? It's a female driver who consents to letting the car be searched. She has a handbag with her.

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: I don't think there's a problem there, because it would not be locked. It would be no different than a brown paper bag in this situation. You would just open up the handbag and look in the handbag. There would be no unreasonableness involved if you were asking to search for a —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What about a man's wallet that's still in his jacket pocket?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: Again, I don't believe that would be unreasonable if you're looking for a particular item. Now, drugs probably would not be located in the wallet.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You mean by allowing — when I consent to have a policeman search my car, I am also consenting to a personal search?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: Well, it would not be a personal search.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It wouldn't? I mean, he reaches in and he grabs my wallet.

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: I would say that under the law that in order to touch a person you have to have some sort of reasonable suspicion. I misunderstood the question. If the wallet were lying on the dashboard, similar to a purse which would be lying on the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I see.

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: -- seat of the car, then that would be permissible.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, but you have already abandoned in — I think in your answer to Justice Kennedy and in your current answer, the proposition that the only determinant of the scope of the search is what you're looking for. That once you -- once you tell the person in the car what you're looking for, that determines the scope. If you're looking for beads you can look in very small containers. If you're looking for elephants you can't.

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: No, the State has not abandoned that. That is —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, but you're using something else in telling me that you can't — I mean, my, I am in the car. My wallet is in the car, in my jacket. My jacket's in the car. But you're saying the consent does not reasonably extend to that, right?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: I think you would have to ask -- because you're saying may I look into your car, you're not saying may I search your person.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Oh, all right.

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: And that's the difference.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I see. And so --

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: And if I wanted to search a person I would ask to consent to search the person.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Likewise, when you say may I look into your car, I assume your answer to Justice Kennedy implied, it does not say may I — may I look into locked trunks in your car, right? It doesn't —

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: I can extend to that based on the factual circumstances of a particular case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Your answer to him was you couldn't pick the lock, right? You could not.

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: Again, what I would say, that it would depend on the facts or circumstances. If I went to the lock and I tried to open it, and I went with a lock pick and the defendant or respondent will consent he was there while I was doing that and there was no termination of consent, or they basically assented to my conduct, then that would be a further consent to --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Oh. Well, what happened with the paper bag here? Was there any objection when —

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: No, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, why don't you use that ground here instead, I mean, you could say no matter what the initial permission was, when there was no objection to looking in the bag, I guess, was that permission to look in the bag?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: Well, under the terms here it was, because they were told —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or does that only work for trunks and not for bags?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: No. They were told in this case, Your Honor, that you may at any time during our search terminate the search on any item. So obviously in this case, on these facts, that would have worked in that situation as well. The fact of the matter is, though, that the lower courts did not go that far. They said that,' simply that once you get the consent it goes to the entire -- only the compartment and no containers within that compartment. And they applied a per se rule in that instance that you could not go any further than looking into the car. Their basic approach was that you could stick your head in and look around for the narcotics, even though they were —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, after they found the narcotics, what use was there in looking in the rest of the car?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: Well, there might have been more narcotics in the car.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: There might have been what?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: At the time they located the narcotics in the car, that gave them probable cause to see if there was narcotics --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yeah, but why did they have to look for something else after they found narcotics?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: But they didn't. In this case they exactly went to the paper bag. And once they found the paper bag, that was it, they were arrested. They did no -- did not do any further search.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: They didn't search any further?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: No, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Because they didn't need to, did they?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: In this case, because of the facts, the respondents were under suspicion for transporting that, those drugs in that paper bag. But just because there was suspicion that that bag contained drugs, they did not have to ask specifically to search that drug, because the search itself, the consent to search itself was valid and they kept within the scope of that search.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why was the search valid?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: Because there was a valid consent. Consent in this case is not at issue. The only person who

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, but isn't it at issue to this extent, the scope of the consent? I mean, I understand it makes — doesn't it make some difference what the officer's question is? If he says, for example, may I look in your back seat, and the driver says sure, does that sure mean he may also open containers in the back seat?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: It can. A general consent can. It depends really on the facts. A general consent may —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what were the facts in this case? What precisely did the officers say and what did the driver respond?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: The facts showed that this officer overheard some conversations at a phone booth --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But -- I understand he has reason for — I just want to know what he said. What were the words that gave rise —

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: He said may I search — we believe you are transporting narcotics. We are searching for — may we search your car for illegal narcotics? And the consent was yes, you may. And it was a very definite request and a very definite answer of yes. There was no hesitation. He was further told, once again, that he did not have to consent, and if he did consent he could terminate that consent at any time if he did not want anyone to go into any part of --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Are these in the findings? Because these facts aren't in that much detail in the briefs.

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: This is the fact that is found by the lower court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I see.

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: In the order of the trial court in the special hearing, this —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And where was the paper bag?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: The paper bag, I believe, was in the, either in the back or the front seat on the floor. Just rolled up, it wasn't sealed, stapled shut, or anything in that nature. It was a --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But it was brown?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: Yes. I did read Acevedo. It was another brown paper bag. So that it was lying on the floor in, basically in plain view. They did not have -- the officer did not have to go through any tearing of any seats, going under any seat --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What if the top of the bag had been glued together so they couldn't get in without tearing it? Could they have torn it?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: They probably could have, had they not destroyed the bag. If it was not that strong of a glue, or any type of a glue —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, they had to -- just, I assume you have to tear the bag to see what's inside it, and look through the hole.

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: There should be no problem on that. It's not really a destruction of property.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No problem which way?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: To be able to do that, because it's just a brown paper bag, easily purchased at any grocery store with any kind of grocery items. It comes along with it. It's not as if it's a suitcase —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But if it were a silk purse it would be different?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: I think there would be an expectation, a reasonable expectation that the police would not rip up your silk purse. You spent some good money for that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So the test isn't whether you have to damage the item to look inside it, but whether you damage it enough so it might bother the owner?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: I believe so. I believe —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He doesn't have to stop and say do you mind if I tear the bag and take a look inside?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: It would help, obviously.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, sure it would help.

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: But I don't think they have to, no.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: A burlap bag is presumably somewhere in between, right?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: It could be. Then again, you might be able to see right through the burlap bag. It depends on how it has been handled.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But what if you can't?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: If it's shut and -- I believe you can. I believe it could be the nature of the item that we're looking at. Again, we're talking about destruction of personal property, and you're really not destroying that personal property because it's just a paper bag or it's just a burlap bag which you can refold over and close up and go on your way.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's my bag. I mean, what do you care how valuable it is? It's my bag. What right do you have to destroy it? I didn't give you any authority to destroy my bag. I don't care how valueless it is. It's mine.

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: But you consented for me to look into it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: To search. I didn't consent to you to destroy a single thing.

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: But it's not a total destruction.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Cheap or expensive, I gave no such consent.

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: But it's not a total destruction. It's merely an opening of the glue or the staples or the rope that is tied around it. We're not talking about total destruction of the property. We're talking about simply being able to open that property.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So you at least wouldn't rip the bag? You wouldn't rip the bag?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: No. We would not attempt to destroy the property whatsoever. We try to get into the property as easily as possible without destroying your personal property, because obviously you're not going -- an individual is not going to consent for the police to destroy their personal property.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Neimand, it's your position that no pressure at all was used against the bag?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: In this case no pressure? No, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No threats or no anything?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: No. The facts do not show that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The man himself knew that he had narcotics?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And he knew that this man was looking for narcotics?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And that if the man found narcotics he was going to jail?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why did he say yes, look?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: There is many reasons.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Sir?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: There are many reasons. He might be bluffing.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I'm listening.

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: He might be bluffing, he might feel that —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He's bluffing?

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: That's right. He might say if I'm cooperating they might not go that far. That's right. Drug dealers are not very smart, because if they were they wouldn't be getting caught. They might have been counseled by their attorney that they can't go that far.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, let's not get into how many are not caught.

turns.name: MICHAEL J. NEIMAND
turns.text: There are many reasons why an individual would consent.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: We'll recess here until 1:00 p.m. Thank you.
